title: Module 3 quiz: Workers, Pages, and edge compute

questions.prompt: What runtime do Cloudflare Workers use?

choices.text: V8 isolates — sub-5ms cold starts, web-standard APIs (fetch, Request/Response, crypto, etc.), WASM support, no `fs` or native modules. Modern frameworks (Hono, Next.js Edge Runtime) target this environment. Node compatibility via the `nodejs_compat` flag covers a growing subset of npm packages.

questions.explanation: V8 isolates make Workers fast and cheap. Web-standard APIs by default; `nodejs_compat` adds polyfills for many Node-style packages.

questions.prompt: Your Worker logs analytics inline before returning the response. Latency is poor. Right fix?

choices.text: Use `ctx.waitUntil(logAnalytics(...))` — fire-and-forget. Response returns immediately; log continues in background. Canonical pattern for non-blocking side effects (analytics, cache updates, audit logs).

questions.explanation: `ctx.waitUntil` is Workers' background-work pattern. Response returns; the promise continues. Use for any side effect that shouldn't block the user response.

questions.prompt: What's one major economic advantage Cloudflare Pages has vs Vercel/Netlify for bandwidth-heavy sites?

choices.text: Free egress. Pages (and R2) don't bill bandwidth; Vercel/Netlify do (with allowances, then per-GB). For media/video/file-heavy sites this can mean thousands per month in savings.

questions.explanation: Free egress is Cloudflare's structural cost advantage on bandwidth-heavy workloads. For light traffic the difference is marginal; for media-heavy sites it can dominate cost.

questions.prompt: Your team stored user session data in KV. Sessions updated in one region aren't visible in another for ~60s. Right fix?

choices.text: Move sessions to D1 (strong consistency on writes). KV is eventually consistent (~60s lag); great for read-only config, bad for anything where stale reads break UX. Pick the consistency model to match the workload.

questions.explanation: KV's eventual consistency is a feature for some workloads (cheap, global) and a bug for others (sessions). D1 for strong-consistent reads after writes; external Postgres for high write throughput.

choices.text: Single-threaded, addressable-by-ID stateful actors. Each instance handles requests sequentially with no race conditions, holds persistent state, supports WebSockets. Designed for many small instances (one per room/document/match). Perfect for real-time coordination.

questions.explanation: DOs are specialized stateful coordinators. Many small instances, each coordinating per-entity state. Not a general DB; a real-time primitive.
